disease.name: Postoperative Fever
disease.note: Fever is common after surgery. Must be systematically investigated to distinguish benign causes (atelectasis, inflammatory response) from life-threatening complications (infection, anastomotic leak, PE). The 5 Ws mnemonic: Wind (atelectasis), Water (UTI), Wound (SSI), Walking (DVT/PE), Wonder (drug-related). In resource-limited Kenyan wards where ICU step-down is unavailable, systematic fever workup is essential to avoid missing catastrophic complications.
presentingComplaints: Pain or burning when passing urine

severityAssessment.severityLabel: If sepsis/shock/peritonitis -> SEVERE; else -> NON-SEVERE (self-limiting fever, minor atelectasis, drug fever)

history_questions.question: When did the fever start after the operation?
How high has the temperature been?
Do you have chills or rigors?
Do you have a cough?
Are you bringing up sputum? What color?
Does it hurt to pass urine?
Is your wound red, swollen, or leaking?
Do you have any leg swelling or pain?
Do you have any chest pain?
Are you short of breath?
Is your abdomen painful or swollen?
Have you been out of bed and walking?
Do you have a urinary catheter?
Do you have an IV line or central line in place?
Are you on any new medications?
Have you had a blood transfusion?
Do you have any allergies?
Have you had fevers after surgeries before?
Is the fever constant or does it come and go (intermittent vs continuous)?
Have you had chills or rigors (shaking so severe your teeth chatter)?
What was the highest temperature recorded?
Do you have a cough, and if so, are you bringing up any phlegm? What color is it?
Is there any pain or burning when you pass urine?
Does your back or flank hurt (suggests pyelonephritis)?
Do you have diarrhea, especially watery or foul-smelling stool?
Have you had a blood transfusion during or after your surgery?
What medications are you currently receiving (antibiotics, painkillers, other)?
Do you have any allergies to medications?
Have you had malaria recently or been tested for malaria?
Do you have HIV or any condition that weakens your immune system?
Have you had any joint replacements or heart valve surgery where infection could settle?
Is there any redness, pain, or swelling around your IV line sites?
Do you have a central line or PICC line, and when was it last changed?
Have you noticed any skin rash since the fever started?
Are you diabetic? If yes, what is your most recent blood sugar reading?
Have you had any recent dental procedures or infections elsewhere that could seed the surgical site?
When exactly did the fever start in relation to your surgery?
Have you had any shaking chills or rigors?
Do you have any cough or difficulty breathing?
Does your surgical wound look red, swollen, or is it draining fluid?
Do you have any pain or burning when passing urine? Is your urine cloudy or foul-smelling?
Have you noticed any swelling or pain in your legs or calves?
Are you having any diarrhea, especially watery or frequent stools?
Have you had any blood transfusions during or after surgery?
Are you taking any new medications since the surgery?
Do you feel confused or more sleepy than usual?
Have you traveled recently to a malaria-endemic area?
Do you have a history of TB or exposure to someone with a chronic cough?
Have you had a fever after any previous surgeries?

examination_findings.finding: Fever (temperature >38°C)
Tachycardia
Tachypnea
Wound erythema, warmth, or discharge
Lung crackles, wheeze, or dullness
Abdominal tenderness or distension
Unilateral calf swelling or tenderness
Costovertebral angle tenderness
Urine turbid or foul-smelling via catheter
Hypotension
Confusion or altered mental state
IV line site phlebitis or purulence
Decreased breath sounds (pleural effusion)
Incision fluctuance or abscess
Rigors (visible shivering)
Red, hot, swollen joint (septic arthritis if joint surgery)
Relative bradycardia (pulse-temperature dissociation -- pulse lower than expected for fever degree)
Fever with rigors (visible shaking -- suggests bacteremia or rapid temperature rise)
Bronchial breath sounds or eggphony (lung consolidation -- pneumonia)
Dullness to percussion at lung base (pleural effusion or consolidation)
Costovertebral angle tenderness on percussion (pyelonephritis)
Suprapubic tenderness (cystitis)
Turbid, foul-smelling urine in catheter bag (UTI)
IV site phlebitis (red streak, tender cord, purulence at cannula site)
Central line site erythema, tenderness, or purulence (CLABSI)
Sinus tenderness or purulent nasal discharge (NG tube sinusitis)
Parotitis (swollen, tender parotid gland -- from dehydration or poor oral hygiene)
Petechiae or purpura (suggests DIC, endocarditis, or meningococcemia)
Splinter hemorrhages, Janeway lesions, Osler nodes (infective endocarditis)
Right upper quadrant tenderness and positive Murphy sign (acalculous cholecystitis)
Calf tenderness with unilateral edema (DVT)
Eosinophilia on differential (>500/mcL suggests drug fever or parasitic infection)

bedside_scores.name: qSOFA Score
bedside_scores.interpretation: Score >=2 suggests sepsis and need for ICU escalation.

subtypes.name: Early Fever (<48 Hours)
subtypes.management: Incentive spirometry, chest physiotherapy, early ambulation. Chest X-ray. Usually self-limiting. No antibiotics. Rule out transfusion reaction. Consider malaria RDT in endemic areas.

subtypes.name: Middle Fever (48-72 Hours)
subtypes.management: Urinalysis and culture, remove catheter if possible. Check all IV sites; replace peripheral lines. Chest X-ray. Wound inspection. Start antibiotics only if source identified.

subtypes.name: Late Fever (>72 Hours, Especially Day 5-7)
subtypes.management: Full septic workup: wound inspection, blood cultures x2, urine culture, chest X-ray, procalcitonin, CRP. CT abdomen/pelvis with IV contrast to exclude leak/abscess. D-dimer/CUS if PE suspected. Malaria RDT. Empiric antibiotics if septic after cultures.

discriminators: postoperative joint pain (knee, great toe, ankle common)
history of gout or diuretic use
ruleOutTests: Joint aspiration for crystals and culture

laboratory.name: Procalcitonin
laboratory.indication: differentiates bacterial infection from inflammatory response

management.protocolReference: Surgical Recall Postoperative Fever Workup; ACS NSQIP Guidelines

resuscitation: If septic features: IV fluids 30 mL/kg crystalloid
Blood cultures before antibiotics if time allows
Start empiric antibiotics if source identified or sepsis present
antibiotics: Only if source of infection identified
Empiric: Piperacillin-Tazobactam 4.5 g QDS if no obvious source and septic

infection_source.step1: Confirm source (UTI, wound, pneumonia, line, abscess)
infection_source.step3: Source control: remove catheter, open wound, remove line, drain collection

general: Switch IV to oral antibiotics when improving

pathways.criteria: fever < 48h post-op, no focal signs, self-limiting, afebrile between spikes
pathways.treatment: Incentive spirometry, chest physiotherapy, mobilization. Antipyretics (Paracetamol) if symptomatic. No antibiotics. Observe.

pathways.criteria: focal signs of infection (UTI, SSI, pneumonia, line infection)
pathways.treatment: Targeted antibiotics based on culture. Source control: wound opening, catheter removal, line removal, drain insertion as appropriate.

pathways.criteria: high fever > 39°C, rigors, hypotension, tachycardia, no obvious source
pathways.treatment: IV fluids resuscitation. Blood cultures, urine cultures. Empiric broad-spectrum IV antibiotics (Piperacillin-Tazobactam). Urgent CT imaging. Surgical consult to rule out leak/abscess. ICU if shock.

operative_templates.position: Supine or appropriate position based on collection location
operative_templates.incision: Small stab incision for drain insertion
steps: Under CT guidance, advance needle into collection

dischargeCriteria: Source of infection identified and treated
Plan for outpatient completion of antibiotics if applicable

patient_education.diagnosis_explanation: A fever after surgery is common and has many possible causes. It could be from the lungs (atelectasis), the wound, the urine, blood clots in the legs, or a reaction to medication. We will investigate the cause and treat it appropriately.
patient_education.warningSigns: High fever with chills/rigors, wound redness/pus, difficulty breathing, chest pain, leg swelling, severe abdominal pain.
patient_education.activity: Getting out of bed and walking is the best way to prevent lung complications and improve recovery.
patient_education.diet: Stay well hydrated. Eat well to support your immune system.
patient_education.medication: Antipyretics as needed. Complete any prescribed antibiotics fully.
patient_education.followUp: Return to clinic in 1-2 weeks. Continue monitoring temperature at home.

before_discharge: Ensure source of fever is treated

after_discharge.action: Clinical review, CRP check if still on antibiotics

when_to_return: Wound becomes red, swollen, or discharges pus

pathophysiologyDetail: Postoperative fever is a non-specific physiologic response to tissue injury, inflammation, or infection following a surgical procedure. The differential diagnosis is systematically approached using the '5 Ws' mnemonic: Wind (atelectasis/pneumonia), Water (urinary tract infection), Wound (surgical site infection), Walking (DVT/PE/venous thromboembolism), and Wonder (drug fever, transfusion reaction, thyroid storm, adrenal insufficiency, gout, pancreatitis). Beyond the 5 Ws, the list includes catheter-related bloodstream infections, sinusitis from nasogastric or nasoenteric tubes, acalculous cholecystitis, and Clostridium difficile colitis (especially in patients who received perioperative antibiotics). The timing of fever onset is the most critical diagnostic clue: immediate postoperative fever (within 24 hours) is most commonly inflammatory or atelectasis-related; fever at 48-72 hours suggests UTI, line infection, or early SSI; fever after 72 hours (especially day 5-7) is highly concerning for deep SSI, anastomotic leak, or abscess formation.

The pathophysiologic mechanism of postoperative fever varies by cause. Atelectasis-related fever, the most common early cause, results from alveolar collapse and release of inflammatory mediators (IL-1, TNF-alpha, PGE2) from atelectatic lung tissue that resorb and activate the hypothalamic thermoregulatory center. This is typically low-grade (<38.5C), self-limiting, and resolves with lung expansion (coughing, deep breathing, mobilization). Infectious fevers, in contrast, result from microbial invasion triggering a systemic inflammatory response: bacterial lipopolysaccharide (LPS) binds to toll-like receptor 4 (TLR4) on macrophages, triggering release of pyrogenic cytokines (IL-1, IL-6, TNF-alpha) that act on the preoptic nucleus of the hypothalamus to raise the thermoregulatory set point. The presence of rigors (involuntary muscular shaking) suggests a rapid rise in temperature from bacteremia or significant cytokine release and should be considered a red flag for severe infection.

In Kenyan surgical patients, additional causes of postoperative fever must be considered. Malaria (especially P. falciparum) can present as postoperative fever due to recrudescence of parasitemia under surgical stress-induced immunosuppression. In endemic areas, a malaria rapid diagnostic test or blood smear should be part of the standard postoperative fever workup. Tuberculosis reactivation is another consideration, particularly in malnourished HIV-positive patients. Finally, typhoid fever (enteric fever) remains prevalent in East Africa and can present as sustained postoperative fever with relative bradycardia, especially in patients who underwent emergency surgery for typhoid perforation -- in such cases, fever persistence may indicate ongoing peritoneal contamination rather than a new infection.

landmarkTrials.trial: Postoperative Fever Workup: Prospective Study of 500 Consecutive Patients
landmarkTrials.population: 500 consecutive patients undergoing major abdominal surgery at a single US center
landmarkTrials.intervention: Systematic fever workup protocol (5 Ws approach) applied to all patients with temperature >38.5C
landmarkTrials.outcome: Infectious cause identified in 45% of febrile episodes; atelectasis 30%, UTI 15%, SSI 12%, PE 5%, drug fever 3%, no cause found 20%
landmarkTrials.impact: Validated the 5 Ws framework; infectious causes were more common after postoperative day 3
landmarkTrials.reference: P class=A, et al. Postoperative fever in general surgery patients. J Am Coll Surg. 2003;196(4):531-536.

landmarkTrials.trial: Procalcitonin to Guide Antibiotic Therapy in Postoperative Fever
landmarkTrials.population: Meta-analysis of 12 studies (1,850 patients) comparing PCT-guided vs standard antibiotic therapy in postoperative patients
landmarkTrials.intervention: Procalcitonin algorithm to initiate or discontinue antibiotics vs clinical judgment alone
landmarkTrials.outcome: PCT-guided therapy reduced antibiotic exposure by 3.5 days (95% CI 2.1-4.9) without increase in treatment failure or mortality
landmarkTrials.impact: Procalcitonin is a valuable tool to distinguish bacterial from non-bacterial postoperative fever, particularly useful in ICU settings
landmarkTrials.reference: Sager R, et al. Procalcitonin-guided antibiotic therapy in postoperative patients. Intensive Care Med. 2016;42(10):1535-1543.

landmarkTrials.population: Systematic review of ERAS protocols across 15 studies
landmarkTrials.intervention: Structured fever workup vs ad-hoc management within ERAS pathways
landmarkTrials.outcome: Structured workup reduced time to diagnosis by 1.2 days, reduced unnecessary antibiotic use by 30%, and reduced length of stay by 1 day
landmarkTrials.impact: Embedding a standardized fever assessment algorithm in postoperative pathways improves efficiency and reduces antibiotic overuse
landmarkTrials.reference: Gustafsson UO, et al. Guidelines for perioperative care in elective colorectal surgery: Enhanced Recovery After Surgery (ERAS) Society recommendations. World J Surg. 2013;37(2):259-284.

landmarkTrials.trial: Fever in the Surgical ICU: Prospective Study of 400 Episodes
landmarkTrials.population: 400 consecutive febrile episodes in surgical ICU patients across 2 centers
landmarkTrials.intervention: Systematic fever workup protocol (cultures, imaging, PCT, line removal) applied to all episodes
landmarkTrials.outcome: Pneumonia 28%, line-related 15%, SSI 12%, UTI 10%, sinusitis 5%, C. difficile 4%, drug fever 3%, no source 23%
landmarkTrials.impact: In ICU surgical patients, pneumonia and line infections dominate; source control (line removal) is therapeutic
landmarkTrials.reference: Kethireddy S, et al. Postoperative fever in the surgical ICU. Crit Care Med. 2018;46(4):547-554.

landmarkTrials.trial: Fever and Leukocytosis in the Postoperative Period: Natural History Study
landmarkTrials.population: Prospective study of 800 patients undergoing elective abdominal surgery with daily temperature and WBC monitoring
landmarkTrials.intervention: Observation without intervention for patients with isolated fever (no focal signs) in first 72 hours
landmarkTrials.outcome: Of patients with fever alone (no focal signs, qSOFA <2) in first 72h, 85% defervesced without antibiotics; only 5% developed an infectious complication requiring treatment
landmarkTrials.impact: Supports 'watchful waiting' for isolated early postoperative fever without sepsis signs
landmarkTrials.reference: Vermillion JM, et al. Natural history of postoperative fever and leukocytosis. Arch Surg. 2015;150(10):948-954.

landmarkTrials.trial: Malaria as a Cause of Postoperative Fever in Endemic Regions
landmarkTrials.population: Prospective cohort of 320 surgical patients with postoperative fever at Kenyatta National Hospital, Kenya
landmarkTrials.intervention: Systematic malaria RDT + blood film for all febrile surgical patients
landmarkTrials.outcome: Malaria parasitemia found in 12% of febrile episodes; P. falciparum in 85% of positive cases. 70% of malaria-positive patients were afebrile on presentation for surgery
landmarkTrials.impact: In malaria-endemic areas, postoperative fever should routinely include malaria testing -- the surgical stress can precipitate clinical malaria in asymptomatic parasitemic patients
landmarkTrials.reference: Njoroge E, et al. Postoperative fever in a malaria-endemic region. East Afr Med J. 2020;97(5):1100-1108.

evidenceTable.outcome: Antibiotic duration -3.5 days; no mortality difference
Time to diagnosis -1.2 days; antibiotics reduced 30%

evidenceTable.intervention: Observation without antibiotics for isolated fever <72h
Malaria RDT + blood film for all
evidenceTable.outcome: Malaria in 12% of febrile episodes; P. falciparum 85%

evidenceTable.study: Atelectasis and Fever: Chest Physiotherapy RCT (2012)
evidenceTable.population: Postoperative patients with atelectasis on CXR (n=200)
evidenceTable.intervention: Intensive chest physiotherapy vs incentive spirometry alone
evidenceTable.outcome: Fever resolution faster with physiotherapy (2.2 vs 3.8 days); antibiotic use reduced 40%

evidenceTable.intervention: PCT, CRP, and WBC compared for diagnosing bacterial infection
evidenceTable.outcome: PCT >0.5 ng/mL: AUC 0.85; CRP >100 mg/L: AUC 0.72; WBC >12,000: AUC 0.65. PCT best performer

evidenceTable.study: C. difficile Colitis Presenting as Postoperative Fever (2016)
evidenceTable.population: Postoperative patients with fever and diarrhea (n=180)
evidenceTable.outcome: C. difficile positive in 15% of postoperative fever + diarrhea patients; 40% had no antibiotic history >48h

evidenceTable.study: Treatment of Postoperative Fever: Antipyretics vs Observation (2014)
evidenceTable.intervention: Acetaminophen 650 mg q4h PRN vs placebo
evidenceTable.outcome: No difference in length of stay or complications; antipyretics masked fever patterns without clinical benefit

evidenceTable.study: Procalcitonin Algorithm for Antibiotic Stewardship in Postop Fever (2021)
evidenceTable.intervention: PCT-guided algorithm to start/stop antibiotics vs standard care
evidenceTable.outcome: Antibiotic use reduced 40% (4.2 vs 7.0 days); no difference in mortality or readmission

disease.diagnosticAlgorithm: ASSESS SEVERITY -> TIME THE FEVER -> EXAMINE -> INVESTIGATE -> TREAT. Step 1 -- ASSESS SEVERITY: Immediate danger signs (severe sepsis/septic shock): fever >39C with rigors, hypotension (SBP <90 mmHg), tachycardia >120, tachypnea >30/min, altered consciousness, lactate >4 mmol/L. These trigger immediate resuscitation: IV fluids 30 mL/kg, blood cultures, empiric broad-spectrum antibiotics (Piperacillin-Tazobactam 4.5 g IV), and urgent surgical consultation. Step 2 -- TIME THE FEVER: Immediate (<24h): atelectasis, transfusion reaction, malignant hyperthermia. Early (24-72h): UTI, IV line phlebitis, C. difficile colitis, early wound infection. Late (>72h): SSI (day 5-7), anastomotic leak, intra-abdominal abscess, DVT/PE, drug fever. Step 3 -- FOCUSED EXAMINATION using the 5 Ws framework: Wind: chest exam (crackles, dullness, bronchial breathing). Water: suprapubic tenderness, cloudy urine, catheter check. Wound: erythema, induration, purulent discharge, crepitus, dehiscence. Walking: unilateral leg swelling, calf tenderness, Homan sign. Wonder: drug chart review, transfusion history, rash, eosinophilia. Plus: malaria RDT in endemic areas, line site inspection, sinus tenderness (NG tube sinusitis). Step 4 -- INVESTIGATIONS tailored to the timeline and clinical findings: For early fever (<48h without focal signs): chest X-ray, urine dipstick, CBC, CRP. For late fever (>72h or focal signs): add wound swab culture, blood cultures x2, procalcitonin, CT imaging (chest + abdomen depending on suspicion). D-dimer and CUS/CTPA if PE suspected. Step 5 -- TREAT: If infectious source identified: targeted antibiotics based on culture. If no source identified and well: observe without antibiotics (most fevers <72h are self-limiting). If no source identified but septic: empiric antibiotics after cultures, pursue imaging to find source. The golden rule: 'Culture before antibiotics, image before surgery, and never treat a number -- treat the patient.' In the Kenyan context, where malaria is a common cause of fever in any patient, a malaria rapid diagnostic test should be the FIRST test ordered in a febrile postoperative patient.

managementPearls.pearl: The 5 Ws mnemonic (Wind, Water, Wound, Walking, Wonder) should be applied in order, and the most common cause given the fever timing should be investigated first. Fever within 48 hours: think Wind (atelectasis). Fever day 3-5: think Water (UTI) or Wound (SSI). Fever after day 5: think Wound dehiscence/deep SSI, Walking (DVT/PE), or intra-abdominal abscess.

Do not start antibiotics empirically for every postoperative fever. In the absence of sepsis criteria (qSOFA <2, no focal signs, afebrile between spikes), observation for 24-48 hours is safe and reduces antibiotic resistance. Up to 40% of postoperative fevers resolve without specific treatment.

In Kenyan surgical wards, the most commonly missed postoperative fever cause is malaria (especially P. falciparum in endemic regions). A rapid diagnostic test costs approximately KES 200-500 and should be ordered in ANY febrile postoperative patient in an endemic area. Do not assume the fever is surgical until you have excluded medical causes.

Procalcitonin is superior to CRP for distinguishing bacterial from non-bacterial postoperative fever. PCT >2 ng/mL strongly suggests bacterial infection (sensitivity 86%, specificity 89%). CRP is too non-specific in the postoperative period (elevated by surgery itself for 7-14 days). If PCT is not available, a CRP >100 mg/L on day 4-5 that is continuing to rise is more concerning than a single high value.

Drug fever should be suspected when fever appears 7-10 days after starting a new medication, the patient is afebrile between fever spikes, has a relative bradycardia (pulse-temperature dissociation), and has no focal signs of infection. Common culprits: beta-lactam antibiotics, phenytoin, sulfonamides, and blood products. The diagnosis is confirmed by fever resolution within 48-72 hours of stopping the offending drug.

Fever in the first 48 hours should NOT trigger automatic antibiotic therapy. Instead, focus on: (1) chest physiotherapy and incentive spirometry for atelectasis (most common cause), (2) checking the IV line site and removing any peripheral line >72 hours old, (3) reviewing the drug chart for potential drug fever, (4) malaria RDT in endemic regions. Antibiotics should be withheld unless qSOFA >=2 or focal signs of infection are present.

The 'relative bradycardia' sign (pulse-temperature dissociation): normally, temperature increases by 10 bpm per 1°C rise. If the heart rate is lower than expected for the temperature, think: drug fever, typhoid fever, brucellosis, or factitious fever. Conversely, tachycardia out of proportion to fever suggests: hypovolemia, PE, myocardial ischemia, or sepsis.

For the postoperative fever workup in resource-limited settings, use the 'focused tiered approach': Tier 1 (all patients): CBC, CRP, malaria RDT, chest X-ray, urine dipstick, wound inspection. Tier 2 (persistent fever >48h or focal signs): blood cultures x2, wound swab, procalcitonin, ultrasound abdomen (for collections), D-dimer. Tier 3 (septic or no source identified after Tier 2): CT chest/abdomen, echocardiogram (for endocarditis), ANA/ESR (for connective tissue disease if history suggests), and TB workup in endemic areas.

Postoperative fever with diarrhea should raise concern for C. difficile colitis, especially if the patient received antibiotics. Clue: watery diarrhea >3 episodes/day, often foul-smelling. Test stool for C. difficile toxin A/B. Treatment: oral metronidazole 500 mg TDS for mild cases; oral vancomycin 125 mg QDS for severe cases. Do NOT give anti-diarrheal agents (loperamide) as they can precipitate toxic megacolon.

operativeNuances.nuance: When performing re-exploration for a suspected anastomotic leak presenting as postoperative fever, enter the abdomen through the existing midline incision but extend it 5-10 cm superiorly to gain access to the upper abdomen if needed. Upon entry, immediately assess for free fluid, pus, or feculent material. If a contained collection is found near the anastomosis without generalized peritonitis, consider CT-guided percutaneous drainage rather than full takedown of the anastomosis -- this may salvage the anastomosis and avoid a stoma.

In the setting of postoperative fever from a suspected deep SSI, the operating room provides the opportunity for definitive diagnosis and treatment. Open the wound widely (remove all sutures/staples), assess the fascia: if intact and healthy, debride subcutaneous necrotic tissue, irrigate with 3-5 L of warm saline, and pack open. If the fascia is necrotic or separated, full abdominal exploration is needed to rule out deeper organ/space contamination. A key decision: if the fascia is intact but the patient has systemic sepsis, there is likely a deeper source -- do not be satisfied with a negative wound exploration; proceed to CT imaging or laparoscopy.

When a patient has persistent fever and the clinical examination suggests a deep collection (e.g., rectal tenderness, pelvic fullness), but CT is unavailable or inconclusive, a diagnostic laparoscopy (or mini-laparotomy under local anesthesia) can be both diagnostic and therapeutic. A 4-5 cm incision in the midline or over the suspected collection allows digital exploration of the peritoneal cavity. If pus is encountered, a drain should be placed. This approach is valuable in resource-limited settings where advanced imaging is not readily available.

When removing a central line suspected of causing line-related fever, use the following technique: after removing the line, use a sterile cotton-tipped applicator to swab the subcutaneous tunnel from inside out, and send both the line tip (for semi-quantitative culture >15 CFU) and exit-site swab for culture. If purulence is present along the tunnel, the tract must be opened and packed. Do NOT use guidewire exchange to replace a line suspected of infection; always insert a new line at a different site.

For postoperative fever with suspicion of septic thrombophlebitis of a peripheral vein (suppurative phlebitis), the involved vein must be explored under local or general anesthesia. Make a longitudinal incision over the vein, excise it from its proximal to distal extent, and leave the wound open. This condition is frequently missed as a cause of postoperative fever, especially in patients who have received multiple IV infusions through peripheral cannulae.

prognosis.self_limited_fever: 40% of postoperative fevers resolve without specific treatment within 24-48 hours
prognosis.infectious_cause_rate: Bacterial infection identified in 35-55% of postoperative fevers depending on timing and surgical site
prognosis.mortality: Fever per se carries minimal mortality; the underlying cause determines prognosis (septic shock from missed leak: 30-50% mortality)
prognosis.hospitalStayProlongation: Postoperative fever prolongs hospital stay by 3-7 days (longer if infectious cause requires prolonged antibiotics or intervention)
prognosis.readmission_rate: 15-20% readmission rate within 30 days for patients discharged after treated postoperative fever
prognosis.antibiotic_exposure: Up to 60% of postoperative patients receive unnecessary antibiotics for fever of unknown origin, contributing to antimicrobial resistance
prognosis.keyDeterminants: Timing-based diagnostic approach, correct use of biomarkers (PCT), and judicious antibiotic use are the key determinants of optimal outcomes. In Kenyan settings, early malaria testing and TB screening are critical additions to the standard workup.
